name: The Hall Is Set
description: Both commanders have confirmed. Solan's hall is ready. The player returns to the monastery to witness the first command-level cross-faction meeting in three hundred years.
accept_text: She reads Cross's small note standing at the window. She reads it twice. Her expression does not change. But she folds it more carefully than she folds most documents — with the specific care of something you're going to keep. 'The handshake,' she says. 'I remember it too.' She doesn't say what it meant. Some things are private even from the player, and this is one of them. She puts the note in her jacket pocket. 'Tell Solan we're coming.' She pauses. 'I've been in command of the Armistice zone for fifteen years. I've made four hundred decisions about where to hold, when to push, what to file and what to ignore, and how to keep my soldiers alive without knowing what the war was for.' She looks at the pass. 'The Grid is returning. The Elder People built it to connect. Two governments have been fighting over the ground it's built into for three hundred years.' She is quiet. 'Whatever Solan's hall produces — it won't end the war. That's not what a meeting between two commanding officers produces. What it produces is: a conversation between two people who have the authority to say, privately, what they actually think.' She pauses. 'I know what I think. I want to know if Cross thinks the same thing.' She straightens. 'Tell Solan the hall is set on our end. We're coming.'
complete_text: The hall is exactly as Solan said it would be — two chairs at a plain table, light from the high windows, the monastery's specific quality of quiet that has been built over centuries by people trying to create space for thought. Solan stands to one side. Cross is already here, having arrived from the north approach. Serel arrives from the south, which is the direction she came from, and they enter the hall at almost exactly the same moment from opposite ends, and they stop, and they look at each other, and then they both look at you. Cross speaks first. 'The handshake,' he says. 'Thirty years ago. I held on because I didn't know what else to do with everything I was feeling and it seemed like the only honest thing available.' Serel is quiet for a moment. 'I held on because I thought: if I let go, we go back to being on opposite sides of a line. And I didn't want to go back yet.' They look at each other across the hall. Then Cross pulls out his chair. Serel pulls out her chair. They sit down. What follows you do not record and do not repeat — Solan specifically asked this of you, and you agreed, and you keep agreements. What you can say: it takes three hours, and when it ends, both of them look older in the specific way that people look older when they've set something down that was very heavy and have had time to feel the absence of the weight. Solan brings tea at the two-hour mark without being asked. Cross thanks him by name. Serel says: I didn't know this room existed. Solan says: I built it for this conversation. Nobody has anything to say after that for a while, and the silence is a good one.
